1: Precise date: tenth year, tenth month, twelfth day — January 587 BCE, during the final siege of Jerusalem. The oracle against Egypt is contemporary with the most intense phase of the Babylonian siege, when Israel's hope in Egyptian intervention was at its highest. Ezekiel speaks against the very power his people are counting on.
2: sim panecha al-paro melek mitzraim ('set your face against Pharaoh king of Egypt'): the sim panim el formula is Ezekiel's distinctive confrontational stance — the prophet's embodied posture of opposition (cf. Ezekiel 6:2; Ezekiel 13:17; Ezekiel 25:2; Ezekiel 28:21). The whole land of Egypt is judged through its king; Pharaoh embodies the national claim to divine status.
3: hatannim haggadol harobetz betoch yeorah ('the great sea dragon/crocodile lying in the midst of his rivers'): tannin (H8577, sea monster/dragon/large reptile) — the chaos-monster vocabulary of Genesis 1:21 (God created the great sea creatures); Psalms 74:13-14 (you crushed the heads of the sea monster; you crushed the heads of Leviathan); Isaiah 27:1. Pharaoh as the embodiment of chaos lying in the Nile. li yeori vaani asitini ('my Nile is mine — I made it'): the creature claiming creator-rights, the ultimate act of creature-hubris. The Nile was treated as divine in Egyptian religion (Hapy, the Nile flood god). YHWH attributes this self-deification directly to Pharaoh as the arrogance that warrants judgment.
4: venatatti chchachim bilichyeycha ('I will put hooks in your jaws'): the great fish hooked and hauled out — a reversal of the dragon's dominion over the waters. The same imagery of divine sovereignty over sea-monsters appears in Job 40:24-25 (can you put a hook in Leviathan's jaw?); YHWH's ability to hook the chaos-dragon is the claim to ultimate sovereignty. qasqaset (H7193, scales/armor-plating of fish/reptile) — the dragon's natural armor is no protection against YHWH.
5: unetattikha el-hamidbar otcha vekhol-degat yeorecha ('I will throw you into the wilderness, you and all the fish of your Nile'): the Egypt-dragon thrown into the desert — the reversal of Egypt's entire civilization which depended on the Nile. al-penei hassadeh tipol ('you will fall in the open fields, not gathered or collected'): proper burial was enormously important in Egyptian culture (the entire elaborate mortuary industry). To lie unburied in the open field, exposed to scavengers, was the ultimate disgrace. lechayyat haaretz ulelof hashamayim ('food to the beasts of the field and birds of the air'): the exact reversal of Egyptian animal veneration — the sacred ibis and crocodile become consumers of Egypt's corpse.
6: veyadefu kol-yoshevei mitzraim ki ani YHWH ('all the inhabitants of Egypt will know that I am the LORD'): the recognition formula applied to a foreign nation — YHWH's self-disclosure extends beyond Israel. yaan heyotam lemishenet qaneh ('because they have been a staff of reed'): mishenet (H4938, staff/support) + qaneh (H7070, reed/cane) — the hollow reed that looks like a staff but splinters when leaned on. The reed staff is the central image of the oracle: Egypt's military alliance is structurally unreliable.
7: beachozam becha vekhappecha ('when they grasped you with their hand'): the literal image of clutching a reed staff — it shatters and pierces the hand that grips it. ushavarta lahem kol-katef ('you shattered/broke for them every shoulder'): the staff that breaks and twists the shoulder of the one leaning on it. The political theology: alliances with Egypt consistently injured Israel rather than supporting it (Isaiah 30:3-7; Ezekiel 31:1-3). The reed staff oracle interprets a consistent historical pattern.
8: The sword oracle against Egypt — heveiti alayich cherev ('I will bring a sword against you'): the Babylonian sword as YHWH's instrument. vechikhrati mimech adam uvehemah ('I will cut off both human and animal'): comprehensive desolation — not just military defeat but demographic and agricultural collapse.
9: vehaytah eretz mitzraim lishemamah uvechorvah ('the land of Egypt will become a desolation and a waste'): shemamah (H8077, desolation/devastation) + chorbah (H2723, ruin/waste) — the covenant-curse pair appearing throughout the OAN sections and in the Jerusalem oracles. The cause: yaan amar yeori li vaani asitiv — 'because he said the Nile is mine and I made it.' The theological arrogance of Ezekiel 29:3 is the explicit grounds of the judgment.
10: mimmigdol seveneh vead gevul kush ('from Migdol to Syene, as far as the border of Cush'): the geographical formula for all of Egypt — from the northeastern delta (Migdol, on the Sinai border) to the southern extreme (Syene = Aswan, at the first cataract). The judgment covers the entire land without exception.
11: arbaim shanah ('forty years'): the wilderness-generation period applied to Egypt — forty years of desolation mirrors Israel's forty years in the wilderness as a generation of judgment. This is a round number for a complete period of divine discipline. Historical records suggest a Babylonian campaign against Egypt under Nebuchadnezzar (568-567 BCE) but the land was not uninhabited for forty literal years; the number carries symbolic weight.
12: vehapitzoti et-mitzraim bagoyim ('I will scatter the Egyptians among the nations'): the same diaspora-language used of Israel's punishment applied to Egypt. The judgment of the nations mirrors the judgment of Israel, demonstrating YHWH's universal sovereignty. No nation is exempt from the same covenant-logic that governs Israel's history.
13: veqibatzti et-mitzraim min-haamim asher-naphutzu shamah ('I will gather the Egyptians from the peoples'): the remarkable restoration promise — even Egypt receives a measure of the return-from-exile that Israel will receive. YHWH's purposes for Egypt include eventual restoration, though not to their former greatness.
14: veshavti et-shevut mitzraim ('I will restore the fortunes of Egypt'): the shuv shevut (H7725 + H7622, 'restore fortunes/restore captivity') formula — the same idiom used for Israel's restoration (Jeremiah 30:3,18; Ezekiel 31:23; Ezekiel 39:25). Egypt will return to Pathros (Pathros = Upper Egypt, the traditional homeland of Egyptian civilization). mamlakhah shephalah tihyeh ('there they will be a lowly/humble kingdom'): shephel (H8217, lowly/humble) — Egypt will survive but will never again be a great power claiming divine status for its river and kings.
15: min-hamamelechet hishphal tihyeh ('it will be the humblest of kingdoms'): the permanent diminishment of Egypt as the eschatological outcome. The proud civilization that claimed self-creation will be preserved in a humble form, no longer posing a spiritual or political threat to Israel through unreliable alliances. This is the 'lowering the proud, lifting the humble' principle (Ezekiel 21:26-27; Luke 1:52-53).
16: velo yihyeh od liveit yisrael lemivtach ('Egypt will no longer be a source of confidence/trust for the house of Israel'): mivtach (H4009, security/confidence/object of trust) — derived from batach (H982, to trust/rely on). The root of Israel's Egyptian alliance-problem was misplaced bitachon — trusting Egypt instead of YHWH (Isaiah 30:1-5; Ezekiel 31:1; Jeremiah 17:5-8). The oracle resolves the problem at its source: Egypt's reduction eliminates the temptation.
17: Latest-dated oracle in Ezekiel: twenty-seventh year, first month, first day — April 571 BCE, some sixteen years after the fall of Jerusalem (586 BCE). The Tyre oracle (Ezekiel 26-28) promised Nebuchadnezzar the plunder of Tyre; the historical Tyre campaign (585-572 BCE) ended without the promised plunder, so YHWH redirects the payment to Egypt.
18: nebuchadretstsar melech bavel he'evid et-cheilo avodah gedolah el-tzor ('Nebuchadnezzar made his army labor strenuously against Tyre'): avodah gedolah ('great labor/service') — the thirteen-year siege of Tyre was one of the most exhausting campaigns in ancient Near Eastern history. kol-rosh muqrach vekhol-kateph murad ('every head was bald and every shoulder was rubbed raw'): vivid imagery of the physical toll — helmets wearing away hair, equipment chafing shoulders. Despite the effort, Tyre's island position meant much of the city's wealth had been evacuated by sea.
19: noten et-eretz mitzraim linebuchadretstsar melech bavel ('I am giving the land of Egypt to Nebuchadnezzar'): YHWH as the sovereign who assigns nations as payment for services rendered to his purposes. The theological point: even pagan military campaigns against other pagan powers serve YHWH's overarching plan. Nebuchadnezzar is 'my servant' (Jeremiah 27:6) — given Egypt as wages.
20: ucheret lo et-eretz mitzraim bescharoh asher-avad bi ('I have given him the land of Egypt as his wage for what he did, because he worked for me'): the astonishing claim — a pagan king 'working for YHWH' without knowing it (cf. Isaiah 44:28-45:1 on Cyrus; Acts 4:27-28 on Herod and Pilate). The divine economy compensates its instruments; even Nebuchadnezzar receives his wage.
21: bayyom hahu atzmichai qeren leveit yisrael ('on that day I will cause a horn to sprout for the house of Israel'): qeren (H7161, horn) — the symbol of royal power and strength (Psalms 92:10; Ezekiel 132:17 'I will cause David's horn to bud'; Luke 1:69 'raised up a horn of salvation in the house of David'). The Egypt oracle, after all its prophecy of judgment, closes with a restoration promise for Israel: the same day Egypt is humbled, Israel's royal power is renewed. eftach-picha betovchem ('I will open your mouth among them'): the recommissioning of Ezekiel's prophetic speech (cf. Ezekiel 3:26-27; Ezekiel 33:22).
